EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 45 mph were measured at US 101 Megler Bridge(ODT76) with a gust of 58 mph. Cape Disappointment (82SW1) had a wind gust of 53 mph with a maximum sustained wind of 44 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool trough of low pressure produced a funnel cloud over the Columbia River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed about 2 miles WNW of the Portland International airport. The observer estimated that it extended 200 to 300 feet below the cloud base. The funnel cloud lasted approximately 5 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the end of June 2013, the Inland Northwest experienced a very moist atmosphere for several days. Constant moistening of the atmosphere from several waves of rainfall and lack of wind or dry air led to surface dewpoints in the 50s to mid-60s and layer precipitable water values between 1.00 and 1.50 inches. Afternoon heating during the afternoon of June 26th fueled low-centroid thunderstorms that quickly produced tropical-like downpours. One of these showers moved over the city of Lewiston leading to brief urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two low-centroid thunderstorms, each producing tropical like downpours, moved through Riverside between 1300 PDT and 1530 PDT.  The intense rainfall caused water, mud, and minor debris to briefly flood an intersection in town.  The nearest observation site, Omak ASOS, recorded 0.76 over a 134 minute period with the most intense rainfall occurring within the second cell.  This cell delivered 0.45 of the total rainfall in a span of 28 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported at Cape Disappointment with sustained wind of 52 mph and gusts to 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific frontal system, followed by strong westerly flow, brought 1 to 2 feet of snow to the mountains. The snow fell mainly at elevation 4000 feet and above. It was the first heavy mountain snow of the season and closed several highway passes across the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to 2 feet of snow fell on the highway to Mt Baker and on the higher elevations of US Highway 2 near Stevens Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme heat lead to a prolonged period of snow-melt along the Cascades.  This allowed the Naches River near Naches to rise above flood stage for several days.  Flood stage for the Naches River is 16 feet, and the river crested at 16.82 feet on May 12th.
Moderate northeast outflow wind in western Whatcom county brought a brief gale to the northern inland waters and also caused brief high wind near Bellingham.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 6 miles northeast of Bellingham measured a wind gust of 65 mph and reported the power was out.

Report as of 8/15/13:  The Colockum Tarps Fire started at approximately 8:00 a.m. on Saturday July 27 in the vicinity of the intersection of Colockum Road and Tarpiscan Road, south of Malaga, Chelan County, Washington. Malaga is near Wenatchee, Washington.||Although there has been significant helicopter, aircraft and ground effort made, it has burned actively, spreading quickly to the south and west for several days. Evacuation notifications occurred in Chelan County (currently has a Level 1 site) and Kittitas County (currently has Level 2 and 1 areas) as the fire grew and conditions evolved.||Many resources including homes, three major powerlines, a natural gas line, two wind farms, timber, water quality, wildlife habitat and range resources have been threatened by this fire.||Three primary residences and several outbuildings in Chelan County and several minor outbuildings in Kittitas County have burned.||The approximately 80,184-acre incident is now 98% contained. Crews continue focusing on patrolling the fire containment line along the western perimeter. Building and reinforcing this containment line through timber canyons has been very challenging and required carefully-planned and executed burn out operations. Although large amounts of smoke have been visible from surrounding communities, the burnout operation went according to plan and is complete. Smoke continues to come from burning activity interior to the perimeter.||Excellent cooperation is occurring between the major fire management partners and the landowners affected by this fire to reduce threats and minimize damage as safely, rapidly and efficiently as possible. ||At 0600 on Sunday, August 11, management of the Colockum Tarps Fire will be taken over by Washington Interagency Incident Management Team #1, lead by Incident Commander Mikael Barnett as they manage the nearby Milepost 10 Fire which started at approximately 11:00 p.m. on Friday August 9. ||Team 4 appreciates the support of everyone who contributed to the successful containment of Colockum Tarps.|||Fire crews are keeping a close eye on the weather. The region is under red-flag warnings for thunderstorm activity, with the potential for dry lightning and erratic winds until 11 p.m. Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Colockum Tarps Incident Commander Mike Barnett's Final Update.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The wildfire burned through 1468 acres of timber, brush, and grass five miles northeast of Leavenworth.  The cause of the fire is still under investigation.  No structures were lost in this fire.

A small wildfire quickly grew to 51 acres along Depot Springs Road in south Spokane County due to breezy winds.  The fire burned through tall grass, meadows, and timber patches.  The fire began between Spangle and Cheney around 1200 PST and was contained by 1600 PST.  Five homes were threatened but none were burned.  Two miles of new fencing was burned in the fire.

The Mile Marker 28 Fire started on Wednesday, July 24, 2013 at approximately 11:00 a.m. The fire is located 15 miles northeast of Goldendale, Washington along Highway 97 and is burning in grasslands, shrub brush, timber, and timber litter. The cause of the fire is unknown at this time and remains under investigation. ||The Washington Interagency Incident Management Team 2 assumed command of the fire at noon on July 25, and will be working cooperatively with the Yakama Nation and the Washington Department of Natural Resources. Other cooperators include the Klickitat County Sheriff���s Office, Bureau of Indian Affairs, Washington State Patrol, Klickitat County Emergency Management, Klickitat County Fire District 7 and 2, the American Red Cross, Bonneville Power Administration, Klickitat County Public Utility District and Washington State Department of Transportation (WSDOT).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Final Update from Incident Commander Nathan Rabe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary trough of low pressure along the Pacific Northwest Coastline resulted in moist, unstable, southwesterly flow over the Inland Northwest.  A shortwave embedded within the flow, triggered thunderstorms during the afternoon hours of August 25, 2013.  The storms originated in the Lower Columbia Basin and quickly moved through the Basin and into the northern mountain regions of Washington and the Idaho Panhandle.  A strong squall line emerged from this activity which created damaging winds resulting in a dust storm and numerous downed trees and power lines.  There was one fatality and one injury at campground from a large tree falling on a tent.  Several other areas experienced property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were downed throughout the county resulting in power outages and some damage to structures.  Power outages were reported in Diamond Lake and Tiger but it was assumed many more power outages occurred in other communities.  There was one fatality and one injury when a 200 foot tall tree uprooted and fell on a tent at the Stagger Inn Campground in the Kaniksu National Forest.

Some trees were uprooted and other were snapped halfway up the trunk.  Trees fell on a combination of roofs, outbuildings, fences, and power lines resulting in multiple power outages.  A large tree was uprooted along Highway 2 in the Deep Creek community.  A large tree fell on a power line in Chattaroy leading to 178 power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure had become established over the Inland Northwest by the 19th of December as a ridge of high pressure anchored over the Eastern Pacific.  Meanwhile, a fetch of subtropical moisture topping the offshore ridge became aimed into the region by late on the 19th and majority of the 20th.  Strong isentropic processes ahead of a stalled warm front led to a period of moderate to heavy snow across portions of Eastern Washington and Northern Idaho.  Most locations east of a line from Republic to Ritzville picked up between two and four inches.  Slightly heavier amounts were observed across the Palouse.  Slick conditions led to numerous car accidents across the region with the worst accident occurring at mile post 229 along Interstate 90.  Ten cars were involved resulting in several injuries and one fatality.  The Washington State Patrol reported to over ninety collisions that afternoon across Spokane, Adams, Whitman, and Stevens Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell between 1400 and 1600 PST leading to multiple accidents during the evening commute across the Spokane Area.  Snowfall amounts were generally between two and four inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate snow, slick conditions, and excessive speed was responsible for a ten car pile up along Interstate 90 seven miles east of Ritzville at 1246 PST.  Eleven people were involved in the crash, four which were injured and one which died on the scene.  Eight of the ten cars were totaled.  Snowfall totals at the time of the accident were generally around an inch.  Roughly an hour later, a car rolled over at mile post 77 along Highway 395, eighteen miles south of Ritzville.  The driver of the vehicle suffered serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm impacted the Inland Northwest in two phases.  During the first phase, a deep trough of low pressure dropped southward from British Columbia into the region.  Ahead of the trough, a warm moist conveyor belt of subtropical moisture delivered a shield of isentropically driven precipitation.  Snow levels were driven upward rising above 3000 feet during the first phase of the storm.  The second phase of the storm occurred on December 2nd as the trough sagged into Eastern Washington.  Snow levels fell to valley floors and numerous convective snow showers developed and pivoted around a nearly stationary 700 millibar low.  A majority of the lowland snow came with the second phase of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Valley locations below 3000 feet across northern Stevens and Pend Oreille Counties received between three and five inches of snow.  Six to fourteen inches of snow fell above 3000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass spilled into the Inland Northwest between the 6th and 7th of December.  The combination of single digit temperatures and winds on the order of 10 to 25 mph with gusts in excess of 40 mph created wind chills between minus eighteen and minus twenty degrees Fahrenheit in the valleys and up to minus fifty degrees Fahrenheit in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills values of -15 to -20 degrees Fahrenheit were observed.  Some mountain locations above 5000 feet observed wind chills as cold as -30 degrees Fahrenheit.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind chills of -8 to -16 degrees Fahrenheit were observed across the Okanogan Valley but scattered observation platforms measured wind chills between -18 to -26 degrees Fahrenheit.  The coldest wind chill of -26 degrees Fahrenheit was observed nine miles west southwest of Wauconda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold High Pressure east of the Cascades combined with offshore winds and clear nights resulted in abnormally cold temperatures for Southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 88 year old woman wandered from her nursing home, became disoriented, and died outdoors. The Cowlitz County Coronor ruled the cause of death Hypothermia. The media reported temperatures of 28 degrees early Friday. Mesonet stations in Longview reported a minimum temperaure of 25 degrees at station AP806 and 27 degrees at LOPW1.

Upslope snow showers produced heavy snowfall for the Washington Blue Mountains Wednesday March 6th through the early morning hours of Thursday March 7th.  Snowfall in inches include (9) in 12 hours at a Mesonet near Ski Bluewood, and (11) at the Touchet SNOTEL.

A combination of freezing fog and moisture from recent rains re-freezing to roadways created icy conditions during the evening of December 14th.  A motorists traveling along State Route 211 lost control of his car, slid off the road, hit a ditch, and flipped the vehicle.  The car eventually came to rest on its tires but the driver was killed in the accident.  Law enforcement determined that the driver was going too fast for the conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A motorists traveling along State Route 211 was thought to be using excessive speed for the icy conditions when his car slid off the road, hit a ditch, and went airborne. The car eventually came to rest on its tires but the driver was killed in the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow started to fall over the Washington Palouse during the late evening hours of December 12th.  Light slushy accumulations along Highway 195 created slick driving conditions.  The combination of icy roads and excessive speeds led to a three vehicle accident and five injuries.  Observation platforms near the accident only measured between a trace and one hundredth by the time of the collision and it is unclear if roads were already icy from antecedent moisture re-freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light slush and excessive speed along Highway 195 led to a three vehicle accident twenty miles north of Colfax.  Five people were injured but there were no fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool upper-level trough over the Inland Northwest coupled with an inverted surface trough axis over the northern mountains of Washington focused numerous convective snow showers through the day.  This led to light to moderate snow accumulations across the Okanogan Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of snow fell at elevations mainly of 3500 feet and higher.  Travel across Sherman Pass was hazardous at times due to the snow accumulations on the roadway.  This met local late season snow criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front plowed through the Inland Northwest during the afternoon hours of April 10th.  Tightening pressure gradients and strong cold advection behind the front led to windy conditions region-wide.  Across northern sections of Garfield County, sustained winds increased between forty and forty-nine miles per hour for several hours between 1500 and 1700 PDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CWOP in Pomeroy measured sustained winds of 43 miles per hour between 1500 and 1600 PDT.  The Alpowa Summit DOT sensor measured sustained winds between 45 and 49 miles per hour between the hours of 1500 and 1800 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pressed through the Inland Northwest during the morning hours of April 29th.  Deep cold advection coupled with tightly packed pressure gradients led to strong winds across all of Eastern Washington with numerous observation platforms measuring sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph and gusts between 40 and 55 mph.  This led to numerous power outages, especially in the northern mountain zones, likely due to downed trees and tree limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spokane ASOS measured sustained winds of 31 mph with gusts to 40 mph.  A tree was blown onto a power line three miles southwest of Chattaroy which resulted in power outages to 56 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms damaged two homes and an underground pipeline near Sammamish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South of Issaquah, a tree fell and struck a woman standing on her porch. The woman suffered a broken hip.   A lightning strike sparked a gas leak in a Sammamish neighborhood, it hit a house, then went through the gutter system and ruptured a buried gas line 100 feet away and causing flammable gas to spew into the air.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant rainfall resulted in damage to cherry crops across several orchards in Grant, Chelan, and Spokane counties.  It was reported that up to approximately fifteen to twenty five percent of the cherry orchards were damaged. The Bing variety of cherries was hardest hit due to severe cracking of fruit. CocoRahs observers across the area reported between 0.25 and 0.50 inches of rainfall.  One helicopter pilot was injured when the low-flying aircraft clipped bird netting and crashed in southern Grant County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to locally heavy rain of 0.25 to 0.50 inches resulted in cherry damage in the GreenBluff area outside Spokane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused local damage and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Silverdale, a tree crashed onto a home injuring the occupant.  There were power outages on other parts of the Kitsap peninsula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary trough of low pressure along the Pacific Northwest Coastline resulted in moist, unstable, southwesterly flow over the Inland Northwest.  A shortwave embedded within the flow, triggered thunderstorms during the afternoon hours of August 25, 2013.  The storms originated in the Lower Columbia Basin and quickly moved through the Basin and into the northern mountain regions of Washington and the Idaho Panhandle.  A strong squall line emerged from this activity which created damaging winds resulting in a dust storm and numerous downed trees and power lines.  There was one fatality and one injury at campground from a large tree falling on a tent.  Several other areas experienced property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were knocked down from strong thunderstorm winds across east and southeastern sections of Othello.  Reports from the utility company included:  power lines down from a fallen tree two miles southeast of Othello, a downed power pole six miles southeast of Othello, and another power pole downed six miles southeast of Warden.  A newspaper also reported at least eight large trees blown down in the town of Lind.  The downed trees resulted in damage to fences and power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm squall resulted in a dust storm across the Columbia Basin.  A trained spotter in Ritzville reported the dust storm and shared pictures which illustrated a wall of dust a couple hundred feet tall coupled with low visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mile Marker 28 Fire started on Wednesday, July 24, 2013 at approximately 11:00 a.m. The fire is located 15 miles northeast of Goldendale, Washington along Highway 97 and is burning in grasslands, shrub brush, timber, and timber litter. The cause of the fire is unknown at this time and remains under investigation.||The Washington Interagency Incident Management Team 2 assumed command of the fire at noon on July 25, and will be working cooperatively with the Yakama Nation and the Washington Department of Natural Resources. Other cooperators include the Klickitat County Sheriff���s Office, Bureau of Indian Affairs, Washington State Patrol, Klickitat County Emergency Management, Klickitat County Fire District 7 and 2, the American Red Cross, Bonneville Power Administration, Klickitat County Public Utility District and Washington State Department of Transportation (WSDOT).

Moist, southerly flow ahead of an area of low pressure trickling northward along the Oregon Coast created a moist, unstable environment over Central Washington.  Shortwaves ejecting from the shortwave trough supplied the necessary lift to initiate numerous strong thunderstorms across the East Slopes of the Cascades and portions of the Western Columbia Basin.  These storms developed during the evening and overnight hours of August 9th and persisted well into the morning hours of August 10th.  Storms dissipated around midday then afternoon heating and a second shortwave triggered another round of strong storms during the afternoon and evening hours of the 10th.  The main threat with these storms was high rain intensity and flash flooding, however one line of storms also delivered damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chelan County PUD reported numerous downed trees and power poles along the southern end of Lake Chelan.  Some apple trees in nearby orchards were also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rumbled over parts of western Washington.  One lightning strike damaged a transformer, which knocked out power to customers in Shoreline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One lightning strike damaged a transformer, which knocked out power to customers in Shoreline.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the town of Harrington, two large trees were split.  One tree was located on the golf course and second on 5th and Sherlock.  No damage was noted to the property.  Widespread two to four inch branches were scattered on ground around town.  The Odessa Agrimet Sensor clocked a peak wind gust of 57 miles per hour.  Several trees were downed in Davenport but no property damage was reported.  One tree did land on a powerline leading to 29 power outages.  A large tree was blown down at the Sherman Cemetary.  A greenhouse was destroyed on a property located seven miles north of Reardan.  The Spring Canyon RAWS located three miles southeast of Coulee Dam clocked a wind gust of 65 miles per hour.  The storm also knocked down multiple trees at the Lakeview Terrace mobile home park.  Two mobile homes were damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were downed within and around Coulee City.  Downed trees blocked roads, damaged vehicles, boat trailers, street signs, and homes.  A large tree fell across State Route 155 closing the highway for over three hours.  The roof was partially torn off at the Coulee Dam Casino.  Several signs were also damaged at the casino.  Due to the roof damage, moderate rains trailing behind the wind resulted in water damage to portions of the casino and offices.  The Spring Canyon RAWS located three miles southeast of Coulee Dam clocked a wind gust of 65 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An useasonably moist atmosphere coupled with strong daytime heating and a disturbance dropping south from British Columbia led to numerous heavy thunderstorms during the afternoon of August 4th.  Slow to nearly stationary storms produced rainfall rates between one to two inches per hour leading to multiple debris flows and areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain across the 2012 Peavine Canyon Burn Scar resulted in a large debris flow down Number One Canyon Road into southwestern portions of Wenatchee.  Upwards of eight inches of mud filled yards, basements, and garages of homes along the road.  Witnesses described a wall of water, mud, rocks, branches, and tree limbs coming down Number One Canyon Road with the sound of rocks scraping the road.  The debris flow reached the intersection of Lars Lane and Number One Canyon Road.  There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An useasonably moist atmosphere coupled with strong daytime heating and a disturbance dropping south from British Columbia led to numerous heavy thunderstorms during the afternoon of August 4th.  Slow to nearly stationary storms produced rainfall rates between one to two inches per hour leading to multiple debris flows and areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Conconully was pounded by heavy rain for almost two hours leading to backed up culverts and mudslides.  One slide was two to three feet deep on Lake Street.  A second slide was two feet deep on Sinlahekin Road.  Three houses on Silver Street had some minor water damage.

A DOT sensor located one mile west of Suncrest clocked a wind gust of 55 mph.  Both sensors experience some degree of sheltering.  One tree did fall on a house but the exact location is unknown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a slow moving thunderstorm fell over the 2013 Colockum Tarps burn scar leading to flash flooding, a massive debris flow, and significant property damage.  One debris flow occurred down Robinson Canyon and across Colockum Road cutting through a home and barn along the way.  The home had water and debris flowing through it and out the front windows.  Residents escaped through the front windows and climbed a tree until water receded.  A large boulder went through the corner of the barn.  Asphalt was removed from Colockum Road between Colockum Creek and Kingsbury Road.  Kingsbury bridge over Colockum Creek also sustained minor damage.  Flash flooding was also observed down the Colockum Creek drainage to the intersection of Tapiscan and Colockum Roads.  Water washed out Colockum Road at mile post 12 making the road impassible.  A mobile home on Tarpiscan Road at the bottom of the drainage was washed off its foundation.  The residents in the mobile home were able to make it to higher ground before it washed away.  Flash flooding was also reported by the public down the Dry Gulch drainage located south of the Colockum Creek drainage.  Flood waters and debris led to the washout of the Dry Gulch Bridge on Colockum Road making the road impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The wildfire was human caused and started near the intersection of Colockum Road and Tarpiscan Road. The fire spread south into neighboring Kittitas County and remained west of the Columbia River. The fire continued to burn into the month of August. Numerous homes, three major power lines, a natural gas line, two wind farms, timber, water quality, wildlife habitat and range resources were all threatened at one point or another. Three primary residences and several outbuildings were burned. Two fire fighters suffered minor injuries.

A negatively tilted shortwave trough lifted through Central Washington during the early afternoon hours of August 29th.  The air mass over the region was rather unstable and moist leading to the development of an isolated rotating supercell.  The storm produced damaging winds in excess of 60 mph as it tracked northward through the Okanogan Valley.  A majority of the damage occurred across eastern sections of Omak, Washington and the adjacent Colville Indian Reservation.  A NWS storm survey indicated the damage was from straight line winds but several reports from the public and images suggested a gustnado may have developed along the leading gust front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous houses across sections of Omak east of Highway 97 between Omak River Road and Vista Vu Drive had shingles torn off the roof and large trees blown down.  One house near the intersection of Kruse Street and West Parkway Street had eight panels of cedar fencing blown down.  A few of the posts which were held down by concrete were snapped in half.  In addition to the wind, damage to the fence was likely due to the neighbors small metal shed which was tossed roughly 150 yards.  Metal roofing from a building at the Omak shooting range was peeled off.  Numerous other trees were blown down in Eastside Park and surrounding area.  Downed trees and damage to structures was also reported in the adjacent Colville Indian Reservation, specifically along Columbia River Road, Cold Springs Road, and North End Omak Lake Road including the Paschal Sherman Indian School.  The public relayed that Boy Scout Camp Bonaparte on Bonaparte Lake north of Wauconda also received significant damage to three buildings and the storm knocked down a swath of trees.  The Kramer RAWS clocked a wind gust to 62 mph before the storm entered city limits of Omak.  A Davis weather station located on private property in Okanogan measured a wind gust to 66 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were downed from thunderstorm winds across southern Douglas County.  Two large trees, estimated to be two feet in diameter, were downed at Desert Canyons Lodge.  In Orondo, three large trees, estimated to be two feet in diameter and 120 feet tall, were knocked into high voltage power lines starting a small fire.  At least 50 apple trees across multiple orchards were also damaged from the high winds.  In Waterville, two downed trees landed on houses along Poplar Street and numerous large limbs were on the ground throughout town.

The water dug three to four foot trenches within the drainage.  The Douglas RAWS reported six tenths of rainfall in an one hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Report as of 8/15/13:  The Colockum Tarps Fire started at approximately 8:00 a.m. on Saturday July 27 in the vicinity of the intersection of Colockum Road and Tarpiscan Road, south of Malaga, Chelan County, Washington. Malaga is near Wenatchee, Washington.||Although there has been significant helicopter, aircraft and ground effort made, it has burned actively, spreading quickly to the south and west for several days. Evacuation notifications occurred in Chelan County (currently has a Level 1 site) and Kittitas County (currently has Level 2 and 1 areas) as the fire grew and conditions evolved.||Many resources including homes, three major powerlines, a natural gas line, two wind farms, timber, water quality, wildlife habitat and range resources have been threatened by this fire.||Three primary residences and several outbuildings in Chelan County and several minor outbuildings in Kittitas County have burned.||The approximately 80,184-acre incident is now 98% contained. Crews continue focusing on patrolling the fire containment line along the western perimeter. Building and reinforcing this containment line through timber canyons has been very challenging and required carefully-planned and executed burn out operations. Although large amounts of smoke have been visible from surrounding communities, the burnout operation went according to plan and is complete. Smoke continues to come from burning activity interior to the perimeter.||Excellent cooperation is occurring between the major fire management partners and the landowners affected by this fire to reduce threats and minimize damage as safely, rapidly and efficiently as possible. ||At 0600 on Sunday, August 11, management of the Colockum Tarps Fire will be taken over by Washington Interagency Incident Management Team #1, lead by Incident Commander Mikael Barnett as they manage the nearby Milepost 10 Fire which started at approximately 11:00 p.m. on Friday August 9. ||Team 4 appreciates the support of everyone who contributed to the successful containment of Colockum Tarps.|||Fire crews are keeping a close eye on the weather. The region is under red-flag warnings for thunderstorm activity, with the potential for dry lightning and erratic winds until 11 p.m. Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Incident Commander Mike Barnett submitted the final update.

The NCSB RAWS station located four miles south southeast of Winthrop clocked a gust of 87 mph.  Several trees were damaged or blown down at the Bear Creek Golf Course but no other damage was reported with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Milepost 10 wildfire was lightning caused and originated one half mile south of Malaga.  The fire burned through 5445 acres of dry grass and steep rocky slopes, cliffs, and benches west of the Columbia River near Rock Island Dam.  Heavy rain from thunderstorms on August 11th assisted firefighters but also led to flash flooding, mudslides, and road washouts disrupting access points.  Level one evacuations were ordered during the fire but no structures were lost.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roughly fifty customers in the Mae Valley subdivision of Moses Lake lost power when strong thunderstorm winds damaged five different power poles holding transmission lines.|As the gust front moved into northern sections of Grant County, law enforcement reported numerous trees and power poles downed in and around Grand Coulee.  Overall, 6500 customers loss power across Grant County during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of June into the month of July saw above average temperatures and dry conditions for much of this period.  The warm and dry conditions quickly resulted in receptive fuel conditions for wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A relatively small fire flared up along the north shore of Lake Wenatchee amongst a group of recreational cabins.  Three of these cabins were destroyed during the blaze, and another sustained major structural damage.  Damage costs were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of July was warmer and drier than normal.  Fuels across the area became receptive to fire very quickly during this time.  Breezy winds on the 27th and 28th also were a contributing factor for rapid fire spread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wildfire was human caused and started near the intersection of Colockum Road and Tarpiscan Road.  The fire spread south into neighboring Kittitas County and remained west of the Columbia River.  The fire continued to burn into the month of August.  Numerous homes, three major power lines, a natural gas line, two wind farms, timber, water quality, wildlife habitat and range resources were all threatened at one point or another.  Three primary residences and several outbuildings were burned.  Two fire fighters suffered minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted shortwave trough of lower pressure interacted with ample amounts of mid-level instability to produce scattered thunderstorms across north-central Washington.  A few of these thunderstorms became severe with large hail reported as the storms tracked northward across the area.

A weak of hot and dry conditions across eastern north-central Washington quickly dried out finer fuels across the region.  Dry fuels were a contributing factor for a pair of wildfires that quickly flared up in early July.  Breezy winds through the Cascade gaps on July 4th also contributed to the rapid fire spread of a wildfire on the north shores of Lake Chelan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire was determined to be human caused and grew up to over 2,000 acres before it was contained. The fire burned in dry grass, brush and trees on steep slopes along the north shores of Lake Chelan near Manson. At one point, the communities of Emerson Acres and Greens Landing and the Antilon Campground were given a level one evacuation notice. No reports of any damaged structures as a result of the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak of hot and dry conditions across eastern north-central Washington quickly dried out finer fuels across the region.  Dry fuels were a contributing factor for a pair of wildfires that quickly flared up in early July.  Breezy winds through the Cascade gaps on July 4th also contributed to the rapid fire spread of a wildfire on the north shores of Lake Chelan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a man-caused wildfire located twenty miles southeast of Keller. Fuels consisted of fallen dead trees, sage, grass and bitter brush. One home and one outbuilding were threatened, but escaped any damage from the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak dry cold front pushed down the Okanogan Valley on the 15th, which resulted in breezy northerly winds.  A prolonged period of dry weather combined with the breezy afternoon winds resulted in good conditions for rapid wildfire spread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a fast moving brush fire that ignited along Highway 20 just west of Okanogan.  The fire was believed to be started by a lawn mower early on the sixteenth; the time of ignition was estimated.  The fire burned through 2,000 acres before being contained on the seventeenth.  Two outbuildings were destroyed.  The fire also burnt down a wooden 115 thousand volt transmission H structure on the Loup transmission line.  This resulted in the breaker opening at the Okanogan substation, which in turn resulted in a loss of power out of the substation into the Methow Valley.  Material damage to Public Utility District assets were estimated at fewer than 20 thousand dollars and the cost of the outage was estimated to be 60 to 70 thousand dollars.  The fire also destroyed one outbuilding (estimated at 10,000 dollars), one hay baler (estimated at 4,000 dollars), one hay swather (estimated at 12,000 dollars), five miles of orchard fence (estimated at 150,000 dollars) and two hundred fruit trees (estimate at 350,000 dollars).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the month of July was warmer and drier than normal.  Only a trace of rainfall had been recorded in Spokane from June 30th through July 20th.  Fuels across the area quickly dried out and became receptive to fire spread.  Breezy southwesterly winds and critically low relative humidities in the single digits set up the ingredients for rapid fire spread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush wire burned twelve to thirteen acres on the West Plains of Spokane County.  The fire started near a mobile home park near Airway Heights and moved east.  The fire jumped across Spotted Road and stopped at Cheatham Road.  Three outbuildings and one abandoned car were burned.  The time of when the fire started and was put out and damage costs associated with the fire were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow produced heavy snow across the South Washington Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals included 38 inches at Sheep Canyon Snotel, 28 inches at Swift Creek Snotel and Surprise Lakes Snotel, and 26 inches at June Lake Snotel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow produced heavy snow across the South Washington Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals included 25 inches at Calamity Snotel and 17 inches at Pepper Creek Snotel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally moist warm front resulted in heavy snowfall from the Upper Columbia Basin, into the Spokane Area, and up into the Northeast Mountains.  Moisture content with this system was on the order of 180 to 200 percent of what is normally observed for the period this system moved through.  A combination of good jet dynamics and strong isentropic ascent provided strong lifting mechanisms for periods of moderate to heavy snowfall rates during the early morning hours on Monday the 7th, which resulted in treacherous driving conditions for commuter traffic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous heavy snowfall reports of five to seven inches were observed around and south of the Colville area.  Snowfall reports were a bit lighter closer to the Canadian border in the range of two to three inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports of four to over five inches were common across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally moist warm front resulted in heavy snowfall from the Upper Columbia Basin, into the Spokane Area, and up into the Northeast Mountains.  Moisture content with this system was on the order of 180 to 200 percent of what is normally observed for the period this system moved through.  A combination of good jet dynamics and strong isentropic ascent provided strong lifting mechanisms for periods of moderate to heavy snowfall rates during the early morning hours on Monday the 7th, which resulted in treacherous driving conditions for commuter traffic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports of five to six inches were common across the area.  Dozens of schools were closed for the morning of the 7th, and numerous slide offs and accidents were reported as the heavy snowfall resulted in a messy morning commute.  A major trouble spot was on Interstate 90 at the Argonne exit where a tanker truck driving west bound jacked-knifed.  Multiple vehicles, including a Spokane Transit Authority (STA) bus, collided into the semi-truck.  Traffic was restricted to only the right lane for at least an hour between 0530PST and 0630PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong warm front forcing and a deepening surface low pressure system tracking into northwest Washington resulted in heavy snowfall across the east slopes of the northern Cascade Mountain Range.  Easterly flow at low levels into the Cascade Mountains resulted in favorable upslope forcing across the east slopes, which enhanced precipitation rates across the area.  Warming temperatures on the morning of the 8th into the afternoon limited snowfall amounts south of Lake Chelan; however, much colder temperatures in the valleys north of Lake Chelan resulted in precipitation falling as all snow for the event.  Heavy snowfall was reported across the Methow Valley with around a foot reported in Winthrop and Mazama.

A potent and moist Pacific storm system slammed into the Inland Northwest from the afternoon hours of February 22nd through midday on the 23rd. A combination of moist isentropic ascent coupled with strong orographic flow resulted in heavy mountain snow across the mountains of Eastern Washington. Snow levels started off near valley floors but eventually rose near 3000 feet as warmer air punched into the region. Valley locations across Northeastern Washington received between one to three inches of snow while eight to twelve inches fell in the mountains.

A potent and moist Pacific storm system slammed into the Inland Northwest from the afternoon hours of February 22nd through midday on the 23rd. A combination of moist isentropic ascent coupled with strong orographic flow resulted in heavy mountain snow across the mountains of Eastern Washington. Snow levels started off near valley floors but eventually rose near 3000 feet as warmer air punched into the region. Valley locations across Northeastern Washington received between one to three inches of snow while eight to twelve inches fell in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Forty-nine Degrees North Ski Resort received nine inches of snow while Mount Spokane reported twelve inches from the storm.  Numerous valley locations including Clayton, Sacheen Lake, and Elk reported between one and three inches and mainly on grassy surfaces.  The exception was along roadways which climbed above elevations of 2300 feet.  Under a heavier snow shower, roughly half an inch of slushy snow accumulated along a small section of US 395 resulting in a brief period of slick driving conditions.  A driver heading northbound on US 395 lost control of his car and crossed the center line, striking a second vehicle resulting in one fatality.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sandy Point Shores reported sustained wind of 40+ mph, with gusts as high as 62 mph, for a few hours.  A power line was downed in southern Whatcom county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several reports of severe weather from wind damage, small hail, and flash flooding hit portions of southeast Washington on September 5th. The strongest estimated wind gust was 61 mph.  Other damage reports include: a tied down single engine airplane flipped over due to wind, and an irrigation rig ripped from the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 34 trees uprooted or snapped as well as a rolled cattle trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system produced heavy snow across the higher terrain of the Waterville Plateau and in the Northeast Mountains. This was followed by a cold front that crossed eastern Washington during the afternoon.  The front spawned a squall line that spanned from the Spokane Area to the Northeast Blue Mountains that produced severe winds in a couple of spots along this line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was injured in an avalanche at Snoqualmie Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pressed through the Inland Northwest during the morning hours of April 29th.  Deep cold advection coupled with tightly packed pressure gradients led to strong winds across all of Eastern Washington with numerous observation platforms measuring sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph and gusts between 40 and 55 mph.  This led to numerous power outages, especially in the northern mountain zones, likely due to downed trees and tree limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most observation platforms in the northeast mountains of Washington experience some degree of sheltering and more then likely do not measure the actual wind gusts that are occurring during a wind storms.  Numerous power outages were observed across the area due to downed trees or tree limbs.  The outages included: 132 outages in Northport, 1634 outages in Colville, and 122 customers in Springdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Water flowed over roads to a depth of 6-10 inches in the vicinity of Heron Drive and Nighthawk Lane as well as flowing around several houses on Morning Dove Lane.  Areas of standing water due to the rise of the water table.

May 10th - May 18th: Temperatures across the area were abnormally warm from May 6th through May 11th with high temperatures in the valleys up into the upper 70s to mid 80s.  This was caused by a strong blocking high pressure system that established itself over the region.  The warm temperatures melted high level snow pack across British Columbia, which resulted in substantial rises to the Okanogan River through the middle portions of May.  Flooding was observed along the river from Oroville to Okanogan in the Okanogan Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service employee surveyed the Okanogan River near Tonasket and found pasture fields, and, to a lesser extent, agricultural fields flooded during peak flow.

Some would become severe that morning.  As the day progressed, the East Slopes of the Cascades area would become more unstable and thunderstorms would develop and migrate over the Yakima and Kittitas Valley's.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable environment coupled with weak forcing along a passing upper-level front led to scattered strong thunderstorms along the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades during the early afternoon hours of June 29th.  Although storm motion was on the order of 20 to 30 miles per hour, cell training across the higher terrain of southeastern Chelan County led to rainfall amounts ranging from one to one and a half inches over the duration of an hour.  The runoff from these storms flowed down Canyons One and Canyon Two into the city of Wenatchee and sewer systems became overwhelmed resulting in flash flooding across southwestern portions of the city.  These storms also fell over portions of the 2012 Peavine burn scar resulting in two known debris flows; one that carried debris from Tronsen Ridge and washed out a section of Upper Tronsen Road and a second that deposited hundreds of trees onto USFS Trails in Devil's Gulch.  Further east closer to the Idaho border, elevated thunderstorms throughout the morning and early afternoon led to numerous lightning strikes and damage to transformers in Spokane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the city of Wenatchee, Miller, Western, Springwater, and McKittrick roads experienced flooding.  Manhole covers were blown off along Miller Street between Millerdale Avenue and Springwater Avenue.  Sections of these roads were closed for roughly one hour as one to two feet of water filled some intersections.  Mud and water could be seen flowing across State Route 28 at the junction of US2 and US97 via a DOT camera on the northern sections of Wenatchee.  The water and mud was estimated to be less then a foot deep.  A CoCoRahs report from one mile west southwest of Wenatchee stated the lower level of their outbuilding was flooded however it is unknown if there was any damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused local damage and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees which caused local power outages and damage.  In Edgewood a tree fell on a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused local damage and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and power lines fell across cars in a parking lot at Green River Community College in Auburn.  Classes were canceled for the rest of the day due to a power outage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The town of Bickleton around 2000ft in elevation was hammered with over a foot of snow in about a 12 hour period.  A low pressure system centered over the Lower Columbia Basin would continue to circulate mostly rain showers across the forecast  area.  However the Bickleton area was cold enough to receive show showers for several hours amounting to reports of 12 to 18 inches of snow by the afternoon of May 22nd. Local schools were cancelled due to the heavy snow.  Power outages were reported between 530AM and noon that day as well.

Although storm motion was on the order of 20 to 30 miles per hour, cell training across the higher terrain of southeastern Chelan County led to rainfall amounts ranging from one to one and a half inches over the duration of an hour.  The runoff from these storms flowed down Canyons One and Canyon Two into the city of Wenatchee and sewer systems became overwhelmed resulting in flash flooding across southwestern portions of the city.  These storms also fell over portions of the 2012 Peavine burn scar resulting in two known debris flows; one that carried debris from Tronsen Ridge and washed out a section of Upper Tronsen Road and a second that deposited hundreds of trees onto USFS Trails in Devil's Gulch.  Further east closer to the Idaho border, elevated thunderstorms throughout the morning and early afternoon led to numerous lightning strikes and damage to transformers in Spokane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High intensity rainfall on the 2012 Peavine burn scar caused a flash flood and debris flow in Tronsen Creek and across Upper Tronsen Road.  The culvert under Upper Tronsen Road was clogged with eight feet of debris and the creek now flows over the road.  Several large boulders were also deposited along the road.  On the eastern periphery of the Peavine burn scar, a large debris flow occurred in Devils Gulch.  A long segment of a USFS trail was wiped out.  Several hundred feet of the trail was covered in downed trees carried by the debris flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pacific storm system moved across the interior Pacific Northwest over the weekend of the 22nd and provided several inches of snow over a 24-48 hour period, mainly above 4000ft. Ski Bluewood reported 22 inches of new snow over a 48 hour period.

A deep low moved NE across northwest Washington.  Strong southerly wind developed over the interior, with strong onshore flow on the coast.  High wind reports were scattered, but a few hundred thousand people lost power in the Seattle area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KSEA ASOS reported a peak gust of 59 mph.  The Evergreen Point floating bridge was closed for a few hours as motorists abandoned their vehicles and attempted to walk (or run) off the bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air from a strong High East of the Cascades combined with strong east winds resulted in extremely cold wind chills for the Washington Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The temperature and wind from the Bonneville Power Administration's weather station at Augspurger Mountain calculate to wind chills of -15 to -25 degrees Fahrenheit.  Temperatures were as low as 3 degrees with east winds 20 mph with gusts to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Wednesday afternoon landslide destroyed a home and damaged others on Whidbey Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Neighbors are cleaning up after a Wednesday afternoon landslide destroyed a home and damaged others on Whidbey Island.  The house that took the brunt of the force was turned almost sideways and is now just inches away from falling into Puget Sound.  Nobody was hurt in the 100-yard slide, but a lot of damage was done to several summer homes.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES CENTRAL CASCADES AND PASSES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low moved NE across northwest Washington.  Strong southerly wind developed over the interior, with strong onshore flow on the coast.  High wind reports were scattered, but a few hundred thousand people lost power in the Seattle area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Cortright Creek Trailhead, Packwood, a hunter was in his tent when the top of an old growth snapped due to the high winds and fell onto the  tent from approximately 100 feet high. The tree top struck the  tent, hitting the hunter directly in the head causing fatal injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving warm front produced snow across the Spokane and Nine Mile Falls areas during the morning hours.  The front stalled over the area with snow accumulations up to around two to three inches reported.  Snow consistency was wet and slushy and resulted in poor traction on area roadways.  Heaviest snowfall occurred during the morning commute when road traffic was at its peak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow fell across the Spokane area during the morning commute.  This resulted in slick driving conditions across the area with 80 vehicle collisions reported.  Numerous vehicles spun out descending out of the Spokane South Hill area; some of these roads were closed off due to the volume of accidents.  Multiple collisions reported on Highway 395 at milepost 175, four miles south of Deer Park; both lanes were blocked with long delays for motorists traveling that route.  Numerous vehicles were stuck traveling southbound on 395 on the Wandermere Hill. No serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving warm front produced snow across the Spokane and Nine Mile Falls areas during the morning hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow during the morning commute resulted in slick driving conditions along Highway 291 near Suncrest.  The Big Sandy hill in this area was closed for several hours due to vehicle spin outs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down near Frederickson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EF1 tornado on the ground for 5 minutes or less. Path  length about 1 mile. Width 75 yards. Max wind speed estimated at 110 mph.  Key damage - tore off part of the roof of businesses at NW Door and again at Encon across the street, and on the Fredrickson Boeing Plant facility blew over several empty rail boxcars, knocked down a Rohn tower and several parking lot lamp posts, blew down over two dozen trees, and about a dozen vehicles with damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system dropped out British Columbia, Canada into northeast Washington.  This created a boundary of cold continental air to the north and maritime polar air from the south.  Valley locations saw a mixture of rain and snow in northeast Washington.  Some mountain valley locations did pick heavy snowfall accumulations, but amounts over the area varied.  Heaviest snowfall accumulations occurred in the mountains.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES CENTRAL CASCADES AND PASSES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous Pacific frontal system, followed by strong westerly flow, brought 1 to 2 feet of snow to the mountains. The snow fell mainly at elevation 4000 feet and above. It was the first heavy mountain snow of the season and closed several highway passes across the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to 2 feet of snow fell on the higher elevations of US Highway 2 near Stevens Pass, the road to Paradise in Mt Rainier National Park, Crystal Mountain highway, and White Pass highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked across southern British Columbia, Canada.  A strong low level jet in the warm sector of the system brought heavy snowfall to the mountains of northeast Washington.  Windy conditions ensued across the Spokane area and in the mountain valleys north of Spokane as an occluded front pushed through in the afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Eastern Washington was under a transitioning weather pattern as high pressure was being broken down by an overrunning warm front.  Freezing fog formed under the presence of the high pressure ridge during the early morning hours.  This produced slick road surfaces for the morning commute.  The warm front moved through in the afternoon with a mixture of precipitation reported from light snow, freezing rain and sleet.  Although this weather system was weak, enough freezing precipitation was produced to result in a thin layer of ice on surfaces across the Spokane area into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of snow, freezing rain and sleet during the afternoon hours resulted in slick road conditions across the area.  Numerous vehicle collisions were reported: a two-car collision near Hatch Street, a two-car collision just north of Hawthorne Road, a one-car collision three miles south of Deer Park, and along Interstate 90 between mileposts 275 and 277.  Multiple slide-offs and two rollover collisions were also reported on Highway 195 between Hatch Street and Mullen Hill Road.

Although this weather system was weak, enough freezing precipitation was produced to result in a thin layer of ice on surfaces across the Spokane area into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing fog resulted in some icy conditions on Interstate 90 between Evergreen and Barker Road at around 0800PST.  The first accident was a pickup truck that had rolled over upside down.  The driver of the vehicle had to be cut out.  Four other vehicles slid on the same patch of ice during the morning hours.  None of the accidents resulted in serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure in place with a moist boundary layer produced prime conditions for radiation fog.  Temperatures dipped into the mid-twenties overnight into the early morning hours with dense fog reported.  The combination of thick fog and freezing temperatures likely resulted in some riming on area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing fog resulted in icy conditions on an overpass along Interstate 90 in Medical Lake.  A pickup truck heading eastbound was driving too fast for the conditions and lost control.  The pickup truck proceeded to swerve into the opposite lane triggering a multiple vehicle collision.  One of the vehicles involved in the collision was a semi-truck that collided into two other vehicles; one of the vehicles struck by the semi-truck resulted in a fatality; eight other injuries were also reported with the accident.  The eastbound lanes of Interstate 90 were blocked just west of the Four Lakes exit for hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong north jet along the British Columbia Coastline drove a cold low pressure system southward from Western Canada into the Inland Northwest on October 27th.  As low pressure deepened over Oregon and an arctic high pressure cell slide down the Continental Divide, strong northeast pressure gradients and cold advection set up across  Eastern Washington and Northern Idaho resulting in strong, damaging winds.  Locations across the Columbia Basin, Northeast Mountains, and Palouse experienced several downed trees and numerous power outages.  At the height of the storm, over eleven thousand customers experienced power outages across Northeastern Washington and Northern Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down blocking highway 206 near mile post 12.  Highway 206 was closed for over 24 hours until crews were able to remove the debris.  At 19th and Arthur on the South Hill, the top of a 50 year Ponderosa Pine was snapped off blocking the street and taking a tangle of cable and phone lines with it.  Numerous power outages were observed across the Spokane Area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds uprooted trees that fell into the Pend Oreille PUD���s 115 kV transmission line near Box Canyon Dam. The outage affected approximately 3,000 customers between Dalkena and Ione.  Other smaller outages occurred throughout the county and kept line crews busy through the night.  A trained spotter reported an uprooted tree five miles west of Newport.  The size of the tree was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system over western Oregon ejected a negatively tilted shortwave trough of lower pressure over the region during the evening hours of September 5th.  Precipitable water values were twice the normal value for early September.  A line of strong thunderstorms tracked across east-central Washington bringing strong winds and heavy rain.  The hardest hit areas were across the Waterville Plateau, the Wenatchee area and east slopes of the northern Cascades.  Strong winds did result in some fallen trees, but the greatest impact was due to the heavy rainfall.  Training thunderstorms and intense rainfall resulted in flash flooding across multiple drainages.  Recent burn scars from 2012 and 2013 wildfires also helped to create a damaging flash flood event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down across a roadway.  Time was estimated based off of radar.  Damage costs were also estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted shortwave trough of lower pressure interacted with some surface based instability of near 1,000 J/KG in northern Oregon to spawn a squall line that traversed northward across eastern Washington.  Strong winds mixed down to the surface along the squall line to created widespread damaging wind gusts.  The squall line could be classified as a Haboob as the strongest winds occurred out ahead of the thunderstorms that kicked up a massive dust cloud.  The Haboob hit the Moses Lake area and Upper Columbia Basin the hardest with widespread wind damage reported.  The dust cloud resulted in a rapid reduction in visibility to near zero in places.  Impacts from the Haboob were felt as far north as Coulee City eastward into Spokane and into Deer Park.  Strong damaging winds and reduced visibility due to dust were reported across these areas as well.

The nasty weather flooded some streets, homes and parking lots, and at one point left 20,000 customers throughout Seattle without power Saturday evening.|Power outages due to broken tree limbs and other causes.|Along with the City Light outages in Seattle, Puget Sound Energy (PSE) reported 2,788 out of power in the Somerset area of Bellevue (cause undetermined); 1,050 out of power in Mount Vernon (mostly restored in the evening); 1,175 customers without power in Juanita (about two-thirds restored); and 805 customers on Bainbridge Island out of power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Westport tide gauge, owned by the National Ocean Service, had 3 hours off and on 40+ sustained with one gust to 60 mph 630-930 PM.|In Moclips, there was damage to several roofs and an old spruce tree was uprooted as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding from Number 2 Canyon blocked a portion of Number 2 Canyon Road above milepost 3.  Flood waters reached as far as western Wenatchee.  Flood waters also damaged homes and property to private residences in the Canyon.  Damage costs were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist, southerly flow ahead of an area of low pressure trickling northward along the Oregon Coast created a moist, unstable environment over Central Washington.  Shortwaves ejecting from the shortwave trough supplied the necessary lift to initiate numerous strong thunderstorms across the East Slopes of the Cascades and portions of the Western Columbia Basin.  These storms developed during the evening and overnight hours of August 9th and persisted well into the morning hours of August 10th.  Storms dissipated around midday then afternoon heating and a second shortwave triggered another round of strong storms during the afternoon and evening hours of the 10th.  The main threat with these storms was high rain intensity and flash flooding, however one line of storms also delivered damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall led to a debris flow across Number Two Canyon Road roughly from mile post three to the end of the pavement around 0000 PST.  The slide left half to one foot of mud and debris, including rocks, over the roadway rendering the road impassible.  The Number One Canyon Near Wenatchee Rain Gauge (NOOW1) received 0.29 of rainfall over a span of 29 minutes.  Runoff incised ditches up to four feet in diameter exposing utilities.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds in Warden were sustained 30 to 35 miles per hour with gusts up to 58 miles per hour as the gust from moved through.  Numerous trees downed and widespread power outages across the Moses Lake area.  Some trees fell on houses and on vehicles.  An outbuilding was destroyed and a fence was damaged by flying debris.  At least six power poles were snapped off near Soap Lake.  Schools across the Moses Lake area were closed the following day due to impacts from the storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of trees falling onto power lines resulting in over a thousand customers losing power.  Damage was reported in Cheney, to Spokane, to Mead and in Deer Park.  A large tree toppled over at 21st Street and High Drive in Spokane on South Hill.  Other trees reported down or snapped off resulting in damage to homes and/or vehicles.  Many tents at the Spokane County Interstate Fair were damaged or flipped over due to the strong winds from the gust front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms hit southeast Washington late in the afternoon.  One of the thunderstorms became severe with 1.00 inch hail.

Post frontal thunderstorms developed across the Northeast Blue Mountains during the late afternoon hours.  Just enough sunshine in the afternoon destabilized the atmosphere to create surface based convective available potential energy of 500 to 1,000 J/KG.  This was just enough energy in a marginally sheared environment for a supercell thunderstorm that produced severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly half an inch in diameter, but there were some hail stones up to 1.5 inches in diameter.  Time of the event was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system over western Oregon ejected a negatively tilted shortwave trough of lower pressure over the region during the evening hours of September 5th.  Precipitable water values were twice the normal value for early September.  A line of strong thunderstorms tracked across east-central Washington bringing strong winds and heavy rain.  The hardest hit areas were across the Waterville Plateau, the Wenatchee area and east slopes of the northern Cascades.  Strong winds did result in some fallen trees, but the greatest impact was due to the heavy rainfall.  Training thunderstorms and intense rainfall resulted in flash flooding across multiple drainages.  Recent burn scars from 2012 and 2013 wildfires also helped to create a damaging flash flood event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: X-NE Road was covered by water and mud for approximately 100 to 150 yards and appeared to be deep in sections.  It was likely that the road was impassable during the flash flood, but there were no reports of impacts during the event.  Timing of the flash flood was estimated by radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood washed out approximately 200 yards of a portion of the eastbound lane of Highway 20 at milepost 266.  One lane was kept open for traffic.  Damage cost were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted shortwave trough of lower pressure interacted with ample amounts of mid-level instability to produce scattered thunderstorms across north-central Washington.  A few of these thunderstorms became severe with large hail reported as the storms tracked northward across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell along Highway 2 and St Andrews Road.  Some wheat crop damage was reported, but the extent of the damage was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low pressure system was situated over central Washington.  Convective showers formed in the cold pool of this weather system and one shower spawned a funnel cloud over the Okanogan Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted between the towns of Molson and Chesaw off of Fields Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted shortwave trough of lower pressure interacted with some surface based instability of near 1,000 J/KG in northern Oregon to spawn a squall line that traversed northward across eastern Washington.  Strong winds mixed down to the surface along the squall line to created widespread damaging wind gusts.  The squall line could be classified as a Haboob as the strongest winds occurred out ahead of the thunderstorms that kicked up a massive dust cloud.  The Haboob hit the Moses Lake area and Upper Columbia Basin the hardest with widespread wind damage reported.  The dust cloud resulted in a rapid reduction in visibility to near zero in places.  Impacts from the Haboob were felt as far north as Coulee City eastward into Spokane and into Deer Park.  Strong damaging winds and reduced visibility due to dust were reported across these areas as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility was reduced to 50 feet in Warden.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large dust cloud engulfed the area reducing visibility to near zero.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities down to below one quarter of a mile in North Spokane due to blowing dust.  One indirect injury reported as a pedestrian was struck by a vehicle due to the poor visibility.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines downed in Othello.  Trees toppled onto homes and fences.  An estimate of forty power poles snapped off.  Schools across the Othello area were closed the following day due to impacts from the storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines downed in Ritzville.  Some trees fell on houses and on outdoor equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the south central portions of Washington, some of which produced severe hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from a thunderstorm destroyed a combine shed, uprooted 5 trees and blew off a roof of a barn.  Winds also knocked out power to 40 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system produced heavy snow across the higher terrain of the Waterville Plateau and in the Northeast Mountains. This was followed by a cold front that crossed eastern Washington during the afternoon.  The front spawned a squall line that spanned from the Spokane Area to the Northeast Blue Mountains that produced severe winds in a couple of spots along this line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line moving across the Newman Lake area destroyed a barn before pushing into the Idaho Panhandle.  Wind speeds were estimated to up to between 55 and 60 miles per hour.  A rough damage estimate was used for the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable environment coupled with weak forcing along a passing upper-level front led to scattered strong thunderstorms along the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades during the early afternoon hours of June 29th.  Although storm motion was on the order of 20 to 30 miles per hour, cell training across the higher terrain of southeastern Chelan County led to rainfall amounts ranging from one to one and a half inches over the duration of an hour.  The runoff from these storms flowed down Canyons One and Canyon Two into the city of Wenatchee and sewer systems became overwhelmed resulting in flash flooding across southwestern portions of the city.  These storms also fell over portions of the 2012 Peavine burn scar resulting in two known debris flows; one that carried debris from Tronsen Ridge and washed out a section of Upper Tronsen Road and a second that deposited hundreds of trees onto USFS Trails in Devil's Gulch.  Further east closer to the Idaho border, elevated thunderstorms throughout the morning and early afternoon led to numerous lightning strikes and damage to transformers in Spokane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a transformer box in an alley behind a residential house, traveled through the lines and blew out a second transformer.  Several homes loss power that afternoon until utility companies were able to replace the transformers.  The transformers were located between Broadway and Boone Streets in west Spokane.  The exact time of the lightning strike was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low pressure system over southwest Oregon ejected a negatively tilted shortwave trough of lower pressure across southeast Washington.  Surface based instability of 500 to 1,000 J/KG and 0 to 6 km shear of 40 knots during the afternoon was enough to spawn a severe thunderstorm that tracked north across the Washington Palouse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 6 inch limb broke off and damaged a fence four miles south of La Crosse, and the power line to a residence was pulled out by the strong winds.  Tree limbs up to six inches in diameter were broken off five miles south-southeast of Winona.  Finally, at least ten trees were blown over in the town of Endicott.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several reports of severe weather from wind damage, small hail, and flash flooding hit portions of southeast Washington on September 5th. The strongest estimated wind gust was 61 mph.  Other damage reports include: a tied down single engine airplane flipped over due to wind, and an irrigation rig ripped from the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines down.
